analogy: Think of "Industries" through the chapter's own opening images: Social and Political Life 60 Unit Four 2024-25 61 Teacher’s Note Equality is a value and right that we have tried to understand in the Social and Political Life series. Over the three years, we have deepened our conceptual understanding of equality. We have distinguished... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Industries is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that social and Political Life 60 Unit Four 2024-25

The text explains that 61 Teacher’s Note Equality is a value and right that we have tried to understand in the Social and Political Life series.

The text explains that over the three years, we have deepened our conceptual understanding of equality.

The text explains that we have distinguished the idea of formal equality from that of substantive equality and the need to move towards establishing the latter.

The text explains that the impact of inequality on access to resources was looked at in the context of women’s access to education.

The text explains that rashsundari Devi and Rokeya Begum’s writings point to women’s struggles to overcome this denial.

The text explains that we have often pointed to the Fundamental Rights enshrined in our Constitution to highlight why equality and the idea of dignity that it contains is crucial to the functioning of democracy in India.

The text explains that this unit looks more closely at the ways in which inequality affects different groups and communities by introducing the concept of marginalisation or exclusion from the mainstream.

The text explains that the Unit focuses on three groups, namely the Adivasis, the Muslims and the Dalits.

The text explains that these three groups have been chosen because the causes that contribute to each group’s marginalisation are different and they sometimes experience marginalisation in different ways.

The text explains that in teaching this unit, the aim should be to help students identify the factors that contribute to marginalisation as well as be able to recognise and empathise with the marginalised.

The text explains that you could help children identify the marginalised communities in your region.

The text explains that in Chapter 5, we look at the experiences of Adivasi and Muslim communities.

The text explains that chapter 6 discusses ways in which the government as well as these communities themselves have tried to address marginalisation through various struggles.

The text explains that the government does this through its law-making function and through different policies and schemes that specifically target these communities as beneficiaries.

The text explains that we have used a variety of pedagogic tools in this unit – data, poems, a storyboard and a case- study.

The text explains that to understand the situation of the Muslim community, we have used data from different sources, which can be analysed in the class.

The text explains that this sense of difference and exclusion leads to communities not having access to resources and opportunities and in their inability to assert their rights.

The text explains that instead, often empires heavily depended on Adivasis for the crucial access to forest resources.

The text explains that for the first time in history, they do not control or have much direct access to the forest territories.

The text explains that adivasis have also lived in areas that are rich in minerals and other natural resources.

The text explains that losing their lands and access to the forest means that tribals lose their main sources of livelihood and food.

The text explains that when Adivasis are displaced from their lands, they lose much more than a source of income.

The text explains that it encompasses issues of power, access to resources and has social and cultural dimensions.

The text explains that the data in the three tables below, derived from different sources, indicate the situation of the Muslim community with regard to basic amenities, literacy and public employment.

The text explains that access to Basic Amenities, 2008–2009 (percentage) Religious Community Pucca House Electricity Tap Water Hindu 65.4 75.2 43.7 Muslim 63.8 67.5 35.8 Christian 69.3 86.2 48.0 Sikh 91.3 96.0 49.3 Source: India Human Development Report 2011: Towards Social Inclusion, Oxford University Press for Institute of Applied Manpower Research, Planning Commission, Government of India, New Delhi, p.

The text explains that source: Social, Economic and Educational Status of the Muslim Community of India, Prime Minister’s High Level Committee Report 2006 Chapter 5: Understanding Marginalisation 2024-25

The text explains that marginalisation results in having a low social status and not having equal access to education and other resources.

The text explains that ghettoisation: A ghetto is an area or locality that is populated largely by members of a particular community.

The text explains that ghettoisation refers to the process that leads to such a situation.

The text explains that this may occur due to various social, cultural and economic reasons.

The text explains that fear or hostility may also compel a community to group together as they feel more secure living amongst their own.

The text explains that often a ‘ghettoised’ community has few options of moving out, which may lead to them becoming alienated from the rest of the society.

The text explains that mainstream: Literally this refers to the main current of a river or stream.

The text explains that in this chapter it is used to refer to a cultural context in which the customs and practices that are followed are those of the dominant community.

The text explains that in connection with this, mainstream is also used to refer to those people or communities that are considered to be at the centre of a society, i.e. often the powerful or dominant group.

The text explains that militarised: An area where the presence of the armed forces is considerable.

The text explains that malnourished: A person who does not get adequate nutrition or food.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Resources and Development, Chapter 5: Industries

prelims_traps: Do not treat 73 Chapter 5: Understanding Marginalisation and GLOSSARY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Industries; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Industries through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 73 Chapter 5: Understanding Marginalisation and GLOSSARY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
